title: Contribute
text:
Ancient Oceans grows with every contribution — and there are many ways to help, whatever your background or expertise.

Contribute or review events — share your knowledge of Earth’s past oceans, or help ensure existing content is accurate and up to date
Provide or fund images — photographs and visual reconstructions bring the deep past to life in ways words alone cannot
Spread the word — simply sharing Ancient Oceans with a colleague, student, or curious friend makes a real difference

All contributions, large and small, are deeply appreciated and help make Ancient Oceans a richer, more complete journey through deep time.
Ready to get involved? Reach out at Lewis.Jones@ucl.ac.uk

title: Ancient Oceans
text:
A journey through deep time
    Ancient Oceans
    4.5 billion years of Earth's ocean history — from the first seas of a molten world to the reefs that face an uncertain future.
    By Lewis A. Jones

title: About
text:
Ancient Oceans is an interactive web experience created by Lewis A. Jones. It’s a scrolling journey that takes you through 4.5 billion years of Earth’s ocean history — from the first seas of a molten world to the reefs that face an uncertain future.
The concept is simple: just scroll. But that single action carries you across deep time, past mass extinctions, vanished seaways, and oceans that once looked nothing like the ones we know today. The unimaginable, it turns out, was once reality.
Built with contributions from expert scientists, Ancient Oceans weaves together storytelling, science, and discovery — a living chronicle of the waters that shaped life on Earth.



The stories behind Earth’s past oceans are best told by those who study them. Ancient Oceans draws on the expertise of scientists working across palaeoceanography, palaeontology, and Earth history — each contributing events, context, and insight that bring the deep past to life for a general audience. Their involvement is what makes Ancient Oceans both accessible and trustworthy.
Without such contributions, Ancient Oceans would surely be a lesser self.

Dr Stephen Pates, University College London

title: Travis Park
text:
Travis Park
Travis Park is a palaeobiologist fascinated by phenotypic evolution, phylogeny, Earth’s physical history, and how these have interacted to produce the diversity of life seen today. His research spans three main topics: 1) sensory biology; 2) macroevolutionary patterns; and 3) evolutionary morphology. He primarily uses marine mammals (e.g. whales, seals, sirenians) as model organisms, drawn to the wide range of ecological and morphological variation present in living species combined with their rich fossil record.
Website: https://www.travisparkpalaeo.com/
